title: How To Build A Weekly SAT Study Plan That Actually Works
excerpt: A practical SAT plan students can stick to without burning out or wasting hours.

sections.heading: Start With A Baseline, Not Guesswork
points: Take one timed diagnostic before planning anything.
Split errors by type: concept gaps, careless mistakes, or timing.
Prioritize the top 2 score blockers first.

sections.heading: Use A 4-Day Weekly SAT Structure
points: Day 1: Math concepts + focused drill.
Day 2: Reading/Writing strategy + timed set.
Day 3: Mixed timed practice + review.
Day 4: Deep error analysis and corrections.

sections.heading: Track Progress The Smart Way
points: Measure accuracy by question type every week.
Retake only missed question categories after review.
Aim for consistency before adding more hours.

cta: SAT scores improve fastest when your plan is specific, consistent, and review-heavy.

title: The 9th-12th Grade College Admissions Roadmap
excerpt: A practical year-by-year guide to help students stay ahead in college admissions without burning out.
imageAttribution: Photo source provided by site owner

sections.heading: 9th Grade: Build Your Foundation
points: Prioritize strong grades and study habits from day one.
Join 1-2 activities you actually care about instead of doing everything.
Start an achievements tracker for awards, projects, and impact.

sections.heading: 10th Grade: Show Direction
points: Go deeper in your main activities and seek meaningful contribution.
Take a baseline SAT/ACT diagnostic to identify weak areas.
Challenge yourself academically while protecting GPA.

sections.heading: 11th Grade: Your Highest-Leverage Year
points: Set a weekly SAT/ACT plan and track progress.
Build a balanced list of reach, target, and likely colleges.
Strengthen recommendation relationships with teachers.

sections.heading: 12th Grade: Execute Calmly
points: Create one master deadline tracker for all applications.
Write essays that show growth, decisions, and impact.
Submit polished applications early whenever possible.

cta: You do not need to be perfect. You need consistency, strategy, and strong execution.
